records.ety: [OE. harowe, harwe, AS. hearge; cf. D. hark rake, G. harke, Icel. herfi harrow, Dan. harve, Sw. harf. √16.]

defs: An implement of agriculture, usually formed of pieces of timber or metal crossing each other, and set with iron or wooden teeth. It is drawn over plowed land to level it and break the clods, to stir the soil and make it fine, or to cover seed when sown.
An obstacle formed by turning an ordinary harrow upside down, the frame being buried.

records.ety: [OE. harowen, harwen; cf. Dan. harve. See Harrow, n.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Harrowed (hr"rd); p. pr. & vb. n. Harrowing.]

defs: To draw a harrow over, as for the purpose of breaking clods and leveling the surface, or for covering seed; as, to harrow land.
quotes: Will he harrow the valleys after thee?

defs: To break or tear, as with a harrow; to wound; to lacerate; to torment or distress; to vex.
quotes: My aged muscles harrowed up with whips.
I could a tale unfold, whose lightest word Would harrow up thy soul.

records.ety: [OF. harau, haro; fr. OHG. hara, hera, herot, or fr. OS. herod hither, akin to E. here.]

defs: Help! Halloo! An exclamation of distress; a call for succor; -- the ancient Norman hue and cry.
quotes: Harrow! alas! here lies my fellow slain.

defs: To pillage; to harry; to oppress.
quotes: Meaning thereby to harrow his people.
